title: Make Art Not Friends
original_prose:
"I'm just a man who's tired of losin'."

— Sturgill Simpson, “Make Art Not Friends,” Sound & Fury



####

INTERNAL MEMORANDUM – MEAT RECLAMATION DIVISIONDoc Ref: MRD-22-4087-CFrom: Office of Operational FlowTo: All Mobile Retrieval Teams (Zones 1–6)Re: Delays in Cadaver Processing

Effective immediately, all Reclamation Units are to reduce corpse dwell time at pickup sites. Complaints have been logged regarding odor saturation, visual decay, and spiritual leakage at multiple high-traffic intersections.

The public must not associate spiritual purity with biological rot.

Reiteration of protocol:

Always smile when on visible routes.

Close all containment doors before acceleration.

Wipe surfaces between loads.

Maintain hymn loop at approved volume.

DO NOT wave at children.

Remember: “Clean hands serve the Greater Flame.”— Compliance Standard, Rev. 14.8



####

The blood hadn’t dried yet.

Not hers. Not mine. Just blood. The kind that lingers longer than it should. It slicked the doorway behind us. It clung to my boots. It breathed.

Kat didn’t speak.

She crouched low in the corner of the corridor, eyes forward, ears tilted like a wolf trying to catch a thread. Her coat—stolen, too big—hung off one shoulder. Her fingers twitched.

Then she moved.

Not toward me. Not toward a weapon. Just one step forward, into the dark, head cocked sideways.

I followed her gaze.

There was a sound. Not mechanical. Not synthed. Not filtered through hymn-filtered comms or signal-scrubbed mics. Organic.

Wet. Small.

A cry.

Kat didn’t look at me.

“They’re babies,” she said.

I opened my mouth. Closed it.

"This is one of those places, isn't it?"

Again, nothing. How can I admit to her what this hell hole is?

"Yo, Captain America... you still with us?"

“Kat,” I said, quietly. “You don’t want to see this.”

I’d been here before. Escort duty. Years ago. I told myself it was just another floor. Another prayer run.

But the smell—antiseptic, sugar, something beneath it I still can’t name.

The women on rails like lullabies for cattle. Fetuses humming. Machines breathing.

I kept my eyes forward. Memorized the hymn. Logged the Spark metrics. Didn’t look left. Didn’t look down.

But I still hear it sometimes.

That’s why I said it.

That stopped her.

She turned, slow.

“This isn’t just a prison, is it.”

I couldn’t lie to her. Not anymore.

She didn’t ask again.

“It’s one of the towers,” she said. “Where they make them. To feed the grid. Just like the stories.”

I nodded. Just once. Nothing in my throat worked.

She looked back down the hall. The crying hadn’t stopped. It wasn’t loud. But it was steady.

“You knew?”

I didn’t answer. My silence said enough. But I felt it—that first real shame. Not for knowing. For never stopping it.

She breathed like she’d been punched in the chest. Then she straightened.

“I don’t care what’s waiting down there,” she said. “We see them. Then we die, if that’s how it ends.”

I looked at her. Not as an enemy. Not as a prisoner I’d failed to break. Just... her.

The only reason I gave a damn whether I lived or died in that moment was standing ten feet ahead of me, spine straight, eyes on fire, coat slipping off her shoulder like she didn’t notice or didn’t care.

If she was going to die—so was I.

Simple as that.

She was my Logos now. Not a command. Not a creed. Just the one truth left breathing.

The drugs cracked me open. The war burned everything clean. But love—

Love was the only thing that ever tried to save my life.

I stepped forward. Not fast. Not loud.

And followed her into the dark.



####

The corridor opened like a wound. Wide. Bright. Surgical.

We stepped through.

It looked like a nursery, if you were insane. If you’d been raised by machines that never touched skin. White walls. Soft lighting. A scent like powdered milk and ethanol.

But the sound—That was human.

Wet breathing. The slow gurgle of amniotic stir. A faint, looping lullaby—Cindy Sheer’s voice, pitch-shifted until it didn’t sound like a song anymore. Just melody as anesthesia.

Kat stopped moving.

They weren’t vats. They were women.

Dozens. Maybe hundreds. Reclined in chrome chairs or hung vertically in hydraulic rigs. Their bodies slack, limbs limp, stomachs swollen and glowing faintly under the skin like something holy had gone wrong.

Their heads were gone.

Clean severance. Neck ports fitted with carbon halos and signal jacks. Tubes in the spine. Electrodes in the womb.

Some of them twitched.

Their hearts still beat. Their lungs still moved. A thin pulse monitor flashed above each one: "Vessel Stable – Yield: Optimal."

The machine didn’t pretend they were people. It didn’t call them mothers. Just vessels.

A display near the entrance listed the optimal parameters for gestation:

Ethnic Profile: Mixed-African / High Clarity

Age at Harvest: 16–22

Emotional Interference Index: Low

Head Mass Reclamation Ratio: Acceptable

SIP Viability: Green Tier (12.4g Spark/infant est.)

Kat didn’t speak.

She walked past the first row like she was counting bodies in a dream. Her boots echoed like gunshots. Every face she’d ever fought for was here, now. And none of them could scream.

I couldn’t look at her.

This was SynRgy’s theology in its purest form:

No thought. Just flesh.No resistance. Just yield.

I heard her whisper it—not to me, not to anyone. Just to the room:

“They were women.”

She fell to her knees.

She didn’t cry right away.

Just sat there, trembling, arms limp at her sides. Then the first sob cracked out of her like something had broken loose behind her ribs. Not loud. Not wild. Just... surrender. The kind of grief that doesn’t ask permission.

“I always knew they were breeding babies,” she whispered. “We all did. It was the only way they could keep up with population collapse. Spark demand. Supply chains. We joked about it. Said it was rumor. Said it was war propaganda.”

Another sob.

“But this... we didn’t know this.”

Her fingers curled against the floor like she wanted to rip it up. “They didn’t just take their bodies. They took their names.”

I moved to her. Slow. Careful. Like I was approaching a wounded animal that might still bite.

She didn’t flinch when I knelt beside her.

Then she exploded.

Her fists hit my chest first. Open palms. Then closed ones. The words came out between the blows like they were being ripped out of her.

“I only ever wanted to be a mom,” she screamed. “That’s it. That’s all. Not a rebel. Not a fucking witch. Just a mom. And they—”

She hit me again. Harder. Fist to cheek. I let it land. My head snapped, blood at the corner of my lip. I didn’t move.

“They turned mothers into machines. You hear me? MOTHERS.”

She pounded my chest. Over and over. “You stood for them. You fucking stood for them. You wore the crest while they did this.”

I took every hit. Every word. I had no defense.

Because she was right.

I didn’t try to stop her. Didn’t try to speak. I just knelt there. Let her scream. Let her break.

Then—her hands collapsed. Her body followed.

She slumped against me. Shaking. Weeping. Her breath catching like it hurt to inhale.

I wrapped my arms around her.

Not to fix it. Not to explain it. Just so she wouldn’t have to fall any further.

And for that moment, she let me hold her.

The sound reached us before we moved.

Wheels. Cheap ones. Plastic on tile. Squeaking like a laugh that forgot how to finish.

Then the whine of something sharper. A mechanical saw chewing through soft flesh. No urgency. No reverence. Just rhythm.

Kat pulled away from me.

She didn’t run. She walked. Fast. Straight. Like she knew exactly what she was about to see and didn’t care if it killed her.

I followed.

Around the corner, the light shifted—harsh and surgical. And there he was.

A man in SynRgy scrubs. Masked. Gloves stained. One hand guiding a scalpel. The other adjusting a clamp on a woman’s abdomen—her womb open, the fetus still half inside.

He didn’t pause. Didn’t flinch. Just lifted the baby free, clamped the cord, and tossed it into a bloody steel cart with three others.

The baby made a noise. A high, helpless wheeze.

The man reached for the next set of tools.

He never made it.

Kat moved like judgment. No scream. No flourish. Just velocity and grief.

She buried her blade in his throat before I even realized she’d drawn it.

He dropped without sound. Just a wet collapse.

She stood over the body, shaking.

I put a hand on her shoulder. She didn’t shrug it off. But she didn’t turn, either.

“We can’t save them,” I said. “Not now.”

She didn’t speak.

“If we stay, we die here. That’s the truth.”

Still nothing.

I stepped beside her, quiet. “Kat. Please. If there’s any chance at all—any hope of getting you out of this city—I’ll take it. But we have to go. Now.”

Finally, she nodded.

Just once.

Kat didn’t turn with me.

She walked to the cart. Quiet. No rage left. Just that stillness that comes after.

She lifted the newest one first. Held it like it was hers. Whispered something I couldn’t hear.

Then she kissed its forehead.

And made it quick.

Again. And again.

Four times in total. No hesitation. No cruelty.

Just mercy. From someone who should’ve had a nursery, not a war.

When she was done, she placed them gently in a clean corner of the cart. Lined up like they were sleeping. No blood on her face. Just salt.

Then she wiped her hands on her coat, turned, and walked toward me.

We turned. Left the steel cart and the soft cries behind us.

And walked toward the last door we hadn’t opened yet.



####

The void again.

Not quiet. Not loud. Just blank.

The sword sat across my lap. Lighter now. And that made it worse.

The Child sat where they always did—knees pulled up, eyes too wide for their face.

“Is that what love looks like?”

I didn’t answer.

They leaned forward, voice softer. “Killing for her?”

Still nothing.

“Letting her kill?”

I nodded. Maybe. I didn’t know. Maybe I didn’t care.

“No,” I said. “That wasn’t love. That was mercy.”

The Child tilted their head.

“What’s the difference?”

“Mercy doesn’t leave survivors.”

They looked at the sword. Then at me.

“But you survived.”

I didn’t speak. Couldn’t.

They smiled. Not cruel. Just sad.

“Why?”

I thought about the blood. The cart. The silence after.

“Because she let me.”

The void held that. Let it ring.

Then the Child whispered:

“Not forever.”

And I was back in the corridor. Hand on the last door.

The Child’s voice still rang behind my eyes:

“What happened next?”

I almost laughed. Almost.

“It got a little weird.”
